name: Dragon-slaying myths
shortDescription: Dragon-slaying myths in Japanese mythology represent the triumph of order over chaos and often involve heroes who demonstrate courage, intelligence, and divine assistance.
description: Dragon-slaying myths are a recurring theme in Japanese mythology, symbolizing the victory of good over evil and the establishment of order in the world. These narratives frequently feature powerful heroes, often with divine ancestry or assistance, who confront and defeat monstrous dragons or serpent-like creatures. The dragons themselves embody chaos, destruction, and primordial forces that threaten the stability of the cosmos or the well-being of humanity. The act of slaying the dragon not only restores balance but also often grants the hero significant power, status, or a valuable artifact. The most prominent example is the myth of Susanoo-no-Mikoto slaying the Yamata no Orochi, an eight-headed serpent. This act not only saved Kushinada-hime (Princess Kushinada) but also led to the discovery of the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi (Grass-Cutting Sword), one of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan. The dragon's defeat also signifies the establishment of civilization and the taming of the natural world. The symbolism extends beyond mere physical combat, representing the hero's inner struggle to overcome personal weaknesses and achieve spiritual enlightenment. The hero's ability to use intelligence and strategy, alongside brute strength, is often highlighted, emphasizing the importance of wisdom in overcoming challenges. These myths also reflect societal values, such as courage, loyalty, and the importance of protecting the vulnerable. The Kusanagi sword itself becomes a symbol of imperial power and divine mandate, further linking the dragon-slaying myth to the legitimacy and authority of the ruling class. The motifs and themes found in Japanese dragon-slaying myths share similarities with those found in other cultures around the world, highlighting the universality of the human struggle against chaos and the enduring appeal of the heroic archetype.

sources.title: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.author: William George Aston
